4219 Zone Expander – DIP Switch Configuration Guide

Back to the Top

Understanding Zone Expander Addressing:

The 4219 Zone Expander uses DIP switches to set its address on the system bus. The address determines which zone numbers the expander will use:

Zone Range Address DIP Switch Settings
Zones 9-16 Address 7 (107) 1=ON, 2=OFF, 3=OFF, 4=OFF, 5=ON, 6=ON, 7=ON, 8=ON
Zones 17-24 Address 8 (108) 1=ON, 2=ON, 3=ON, 4=ON, 5=OFF, 6=ON, 7=ON, 8=ON
Zones 25-32 Address 9 (109) 1=ON, 2=OFF, 3=ON, 4=ON, 5=OFF, 6=ON, 7=ON, 8=ON
Zones 33-40 Address 10 (110) 1=ON, 2=ON, 3=OFF, 4=ON, 5=OFF, 6=ON, 7=ON, 8=ON

Important Notes:

DIP Switch 1 should ALWAYS be ON

DIP Switch 7 should ALWAYS be ON (for Normally Closed loops)

DIP Switch 8 should ALWAYS be ON

Switches 2-6 determine the address (see table above)

For our training system, we use Address 7 (107) for zones 9-16

The first zone expander ALWAYS starts at zone 9 (Address 7)

Tuxedo Touch AUI Keypad – Configuration Guide

Back to the Top

Understanding AUI (Advanced User Interface) Keypads

The Tuxedo Touch provides enhanced control and automation capabilities for the Vista 20P system.

Addressing: During initial boot-up, select address 1 on the touchscreen. Vista 20P supports up to 4 AUI keypads using addresses 1, 2, 5, or 6.

Programming: Enable AUI support in the panel using field *189 (set to 1500). The Tuxedo Touch connects to the ECP bus but requires more power than standard keypads.

Z-Wave Integration: The Tuxedo Touch serves as a Z-Wave controller for thermostats, lights, and locks. Add devices through Automation → Z-Wave Device Management → Add Device.

Training Value: Demonstrates advanced user interfaces and home automation capabilities beyond traditional security functions.

4219 Zone Expander – Practical Applications

Back to the Top

Why Use a 4219 Zone Expander?

The 4219 Zone Expander is a critical component when you need to add more hardwired zones beyond what the main panel supports. Here are common scenarios where a 4219 is essential:

  • Main Panel Zone Expansion: When you’ve used up all 8 hardwired zones on your main Vista 20P panel but need additional hardwired protection points in the residence.
  • Detached Buildings: For protecting detached structures like garages or sheds – simply run a 4-wire cable from the main house to the garage, install a 4219 in the garage, and connect hardwired sensors locally without running individual zone wires back to the main panel.
  • Zone Concentration: When you have multiple protection points in a specific area of a large home (like a master suite with multiple doors/windows), a 4219 can be installed nearby to minimize wiring runs.
  • Multiple Expanders: For larger installations, multiple 4219s can be added to the system (using zones 9-16, 17-24, etc.), allowing for significant expansion of hardwired capacity.

Training Context Note: In this training program, we’re including a 4219 Zone Expander primarily for demonstration purposes. In a real-world installation with this few zones (only using zones 1-3 on the main panel), you typically wouldn’t install a 4219 as there are still 5 unused hardwired zones available on the main panel. The 4219 is included here to demonstrate how zone numbering shifts when an expander is present and to provide hands-on experience with this common expansion device.

Important Note: When using a 4219, wireless zones will start at zone 17 instead of zone 9, as zones 9-16 are now reserved for the zone expander.

Zone Distribution Explanation

Back to the Top

Understanding Zone Distribution

Proper zone distribution is critical for Vista 20P programming. The way zones are assigned depends on your hardware configuration:

  • Basic Setup (No Zone Expander): Hardwired zones use zones 1-8 on the panel, and wireless zones start at zone 9.
  • With Zone Expander (4219): Hardwired zones use zones 1-8 on the panel, zone expander uses zones 9-16, and wireless zones start at zone 17.
  • Multiple Zone Expanders: Each additional zone expander will shift wireless zones further up in the sequence.

Important Note: Zone expanders cannot be assigned to zone 4 or any other panel zone (1-8). They must use zones 9-16.

Cross-Zoning – Practical Applications

Back to the Top

What is Cross-Zoning and Why Use It?

Cross-zoning is a powerful feature that requires two separate zones to be triggered within a specified time window before generating an alarm. This significantly reduces false alarms while maintaining security.

Real-World Applications:

  • Garage Protection: Garages are notorious for false alarms due to mice, temperature changes, or moving objects. Installing two motion detectors at different angles and cross-zoning them ensures that small animals triggering one sensor won’t cause an alarm unless the second sensor also detects movement.
  • Problematic Areas: For zones that have historically generated false alarms (near HVAC vents, areas with shifting sunlight, etc.), cross-zoning provides an excellent solution without compromising security.
  • High-Value Areas: For areas containing valuables, cross-zoning two different types of sensors (e.g., a motion detector and a glass break detector) creates a highly secure zone that’s also resistant to false alarms.
  • Large Open Spaces: In warehouses or large living areas, cross-zoning multiple motion detectors ensures that environmental factors affecting one area won’t trigger false alarms.

In This Training: We’re cross-zoning the wired motion sensor on the panel (zone 03) with the wired motion sensor on the zone expander (zone 09) to demonstrate how this feature works across different connection points. In a real installation, these would typically be placed in the same area to monitor the same space from different angles.

Wireless Key Fob – Configuration & Benefits

Back to the Top

Understanding Key Fob Programming

The wireless key fob provides convenient remote control of your security system from anywhere within wireless range.

ButtonZone NumberZone TypeFunction
Button A4920 – Arm-StayArms the system in stay mode for when occupants are home
Button B5021 – Arm-AwayArms the system in away mode when premises are vacant
Button C5122 – DisarmDisarms the system with a single button press
Button D5206 – 24-Hour Silent PanicSends silent alarm to central station

Enrollment Process: Each button must be programmed separately in *56 mode. When you see the serial number displayed, press the corresponding key fob button. After programming all buttons, finalize the key fob enrollment with: [Master Code] + [8] + [02] + [#] + [4] + [49] + [*]

User Experience: Key fobs eliminate the need to rush to a keypad during entry/exit delays and provide a simple way for users who struggle with remembering codes.

Programming Purpose Reference

Back to the Top
Programming Field Value(s) Set Reason (Why It Matters)
Initial Setup Address 16, 4112+800 When first powering up the system, the keypad must be addressed to 16 by holding [1] and [3] buttons. Then enter programming mode using the default installer code 4112 followed by [8] [0] [0].
*20 [1] [9] [4] [3] This is the installer code. McKee Security uses “1943” across all panels for consistency, so any technician can access programming if needed in the future.
*29 [1] This enables the cellular communicator. This is required when the system will report alarms via a GSM module instead of a landline. Cellular is the standard communication method for modern installations.
*34, *35 [6060] These set the entry and exit delays for both partitions. McKee’s standard timing is 60 seconds for each partition, allowing users enough time to enter/exit without false alarms, but still ensuring quick response if needed.
*50 [0] This sets the burglary delay to 0, which removes any delay in reporting burglary alarms to the central station. This ensures immediate notification when an alarm is triggered.
*59 [0] This disables the exit error alarm report code. When set to 0, the system will not report exit errors to the central station, which can reduce false alarms during the exit process.
*63 [1] [1] This enables low battery report codes for both partitions. When set to 11, the system will report low battery conditions to the central station for both partition 1 and 2.
*65 [1] [1] [0] This enables open report codes. When set to 110, the system will report when the system is disarmed (opened) to the central station for both partitions.
*66 [1] [1] [1] [1] [0] [0] This enables arm away/stay report codes. When set to 111100, the system will report when the system is armed in away or stay mode to the central station for both partitions.
*67 [1] [1] This enables RF transmitter low battery report codes for both partitions. When set to 11, the system will report when wireless devices have low batteries to the central station.
*68 [1] [1] This enables cancel report codes for both partitions. When set to 11, the system will report when an alarm is canceled by a user to the central station.
*70 [1] This enables restore reports to be sent to the central station. When set to 1, the system will report when troubles have been resolved, such as power being restored or batteries replaced.
*74 [1] [1] This enables low battery restore report codes for both partitions. When set to 11, the system will report when low battery conditions have been resolved to the central station.
*75 [1] [1] This enables RF transmitter low battery restore report codes for both partitions. When set to 11, the system will report when wireless device low battery conditions have been resolved to the central station.
*81 Option [04], [0] [3],[09] This configures cross-zoning pairs through the menu modes. Option 04 is for cross zones, and 03,09 pairs the wired motion sensor (zone 3) with the wired motion sensor (zone 9), meaning both must trigger within the cross-zone time window to generate an alarm.
*84 [1] This enables auto stay arming. When set to 1, the system will automatically switch to stay mode if no entry/exit door is opened during the exit delay when arming in away mode.
*85 [7] This sets the cross-zone timer to 7, which establishes a 3-minute window in which two cross-zoned sensors must trigger to generate an alarm — reducing false alarms from pets, HVAC, or movement in isolated areas.
*86 [0] This disables the cancel verify keypad display. When set to 0, the system will not display a verification message on the keypad when an alarm is canceled, which simplifies the user experience.
*90 [#] [1] [5] This enables event logging, allowing technicians to later review historical events like arming/disarming, alarms, faults, and troubles. Crucial for troubleshooting and verifying system activity.
*189 [1] [5] [0] [0] This field enables AUI (Advanced User Interface) keypads or online keypads. Set to 1500 when utilizing services like Total Connect 2.0 for remote access and control. This allows the system to communicate with smartphone apps and web interfaces.
Tuxedo Touch Address [1] The Tuxedo Touch keypad requires special addressing during initial boot-up. The Vista 20P supports up to 4 AUI keypads with addresses 1, 2, 5, or 6. For our training system, we use address [1]. The keypad must be configured after programming the panel with field * 189 set to [1] [5] [0] [0] to enable AUI support.
Z-Wave Integration Via Tuxedo Touch The Tuxedo Touch serves as a Z-Wave controller, allowing integration with compatible devices like thermostats, lights, and locks. Z-Wave devices are added through the Tuxedo Touch interface by navigating to Automation → Z-Wave Device Management → Add (Include) a Device. This extends the security system’s capabilities into home automation.
*56 See table above * 56 is the heart of zone programming. Each device (door contact, motion sensor, smoke detector, etc.) gets assigned to a specific zone with a “zone type” that tells the system how to treat that device (entry door, interior motion, etc.).
*57 Away: [03], Stay: [04] This assigns quick arm keys. These let the user arm the system directly from the keypad in either Away mode ([0] [3] – leaving the house) or Stay mode ([0] [4] – staying inside). Programming sequence: Select button to program → Press * * → Enter function code (Away=[0] [3], Stay=[0] [4]) → Press * → Enter [0] to exit.
*99 N/A This exits programming mode and returns the system to normal operation. Always use this command to properly exit programming rather than just powering down the system.
User Codes [Master Code] + [8] + [User #] + [New Code] This programs user codes that allow different people to arm/disarm the system. Important: User codes are programmed AFTER exiting programming mode ([*] [9] [9]). Each user gets their own code, which can be tracked in the event log.

Common Programming Pitfalls

Back to the Top
Issue Solution
Keypad shows “FC” error Communication failure – verify AlarmNet settings and signal strength
Wireless devices not enrolling Ensure 6160RF keypad is properly addressed and check device loop number
Zone expander not recognized Check DIP switch settings and ensure it’s using zones 9-16, not panel zones 1-8
System won’t arm Check for faulted zones or low battery conditions
Cellular communication issues Verify GSM signal strength and proper AlarmNet registration
Tuxedo Touch not communicating Verify field *189 is set to 1500 and check that the keypad is addressed correctly (1, 2, 5, or 6)
Z-Wave devices not including Ensure Z-Wave device is in inclusion mode and within range of the Tuxedo Touch; reset Z-Wave network if necessary
Tuxedo Touch shows “Please Wait” indefinitely Power cycle the Tuxedo Touch and verify proper bus connections (data in/out and power)

AlarmNet 360 Account Setup – Detailed Reference

Back to the Top
Setup Step Value/Action Explanation
Lanvac.Mobi Access Login: Contact administrator Lanvac is the central station that will monitor the alarm system. The first step is to create an account in their system before setting up the AlarmNet communicator.
Account Number Format Must start with “O”, have 4 digits, and end with a number This format is required by Lanvac’s system. The “O” prefix identifies it as a specific account type, and the 4-digit format ensures compatibility with their monitoring software.
Account Testing Put account on test for the day This prevents false alarms from being dispatched during the installation and programming process. The central station will log signals but not dispatch authorities.
Communicator Configuration Type: “AlarmNet Communicator”
Option: “Partial Cancel” checked		 This tells the central station what type of communication device is being used and enables the partial cancel feature, which allows users to cancel alarms for certain zone types while maintaining monitoring for others.
Processing Time Up to 30 minutes The Lanvac system may take up to 30 minutes to fully process and activate the new communicator settings. This is normal and should be accounted for in your installation timeline.
AlarmNet 360 Login Login: Contact administrator
Password: Contact administrator		 These are the company credentials for accessing the AlarmNet 360 portal. All technicians use the same login to ensure consistent account management and tracking. Note: The password is not shown here for security reasons – please contact your system administrator for the current password.
Account Identification 90-84-XXXX This is the unique identifier for the alarm system in the format of “City Code, Central Station, Account Number”. This specific format ensures proper routing of alarm signals to the correct central station.
City Code 90 The city code “90” is assigned to your geographic region. This helps the central station identify the general location of the alarm system for dispatch purposes.
Central Station 84 The code “84” identifies the specific central monitoring station that will receive and process alarm signals from this system. Each monitoring center has a unique identifier.
Account Number XXXX This is the unique customer account number assigned to this specific installation. The central station uses this to identify exactly which customer is reporting an alarm.
Communicator Identifiers MAC & CRC These unique identifiers are printed on the cellular communicator box. The MAC is the hardware address of the communicator, while the CRC is a verification code. Both are required to register the device on the AlarmNet network.
Service Levels Security / Smart Security / Smart Home These tiers determine the functionality available to the customer. “Security” provides basic monitoring only. “Smart Security” adds Total Connect 2.0 remote access without home automation. “Smart Home” includes both Total Connect 2.0 and home automation capabilities.
Registration Process Register → Send data to device This two-step process first registers the communicator in the AlarmNet database, then pushes the configuration data to the physical device. Both steps must be completed in sequence for proper operation.

AlarmNet 360 Setup – Practical Information

Back to the Top

Understanding the Complete Setup Process

Setting up alarm monitoring involves two separate but connected systems: Lanvac (the central station) and AlarmNet 360 (the communicator management platform). Here’s what you need to know:

Lanvac.Mobi Process:

  • Central Station Setup: Lanvac is the monitoring center that will receive and respond to alarm signals. Creating an account here is the first step in establishing monitoring service.
  • Account Number Format: The “O” prefix followed by 4 digits (ending in a number) is Lanvac’s required format. This unique identifier will be used to route signals to the correct customer account.
  • Testing Period: Putting the account on test is critical during installation to prevent false dispatches while still allowing you to verify signal transmission.
  • Communicator Configuration: Setting the communicator type to “AlarmNet Communicator” tells Lanvac’s system what protocol to expect for incoming signals. The “Partial Cancel” option is a customer-friendly feature that allows homeowners to cancel certain types of alarms.
  • Processing Delay: The 30-minute processing time is normal and represents the time needed for Lanvac’s systems to fully integrate the new account information.

AlarmNet 360 Portal:

  • Communicator Management: AlarmNet 360 is Honeywell/Resideo’s cloud-based platform for managing security systems and communicators. It provides a centralized interface for account creation, device registration, and service management.
  • Account Creation Process: The sequence “Devices → Programming → New Account → Security/Fire” navigates through the account creation wizard. This path allows you to set up a new security or fire monitoring account with the proper parameters for central station monitoring.
  • Account Number Format: The “90-84-XXXX” format combines three critical identifiers (City-CS-Account) into a single string that uniquely identifies this customer in the monitoring network.
  • Region Selection: Selecting “Canada” ensures proper compliance with Canadian telecommunications regulations and sets appropriate default settings for the region.

Communicator Registration:

  • MAC and CRC: These values are unique to each cellular communicator and cannot be changed. They serve as the “digital fingerprint” of the device on the AlarmNet network.
  • Registration Status: When properly registered, the REG (Registration) light on the communicator will turn OFF, indicating successful connection to the AlarmNet network.
  • Manual Registration: If the communicator doesn’t register automatically after sending data, pressing the button on the communicator 3 times will initiate a manual registration attempt.
  • Visual Confirmation: In the AlarmNet 360 portal, the account number will change from red (unregistered) to green (registered) when the process is successful.

Service Level Selection:

  • Security: Basic monitoring service with no remote access. Signals are sent to the central station, but the customer cannot interact with their system remotely.
  • Smart Security: Includes Total Connect 2.0 access, allowing customers to arm/disarm, check status, and receive notifications on their mobile devices. Does not include control of smart home devices.
  • Smart Home: The complete package, including both alarm monitoring and home automation capabilities. Allows customers to control compatible Z-Wave devices, create scenes, and set schedules through the Total Connect 2.0 app.

Troubleshooting Tips: If the account doesn’t register properly, verify the MAC and CRC values, check that the communicator has adequate cellular signal strength, and ensure the Vista panel is properly programmed for cellular communication (*29=1). Also verify that the Lanvac account setup has been completed and the 30-minute processing time has elapsed.

Common Setup Issues

Back to the Top
Issue Solution
Account remains red in AlarmNet 360 Verify MAC/CRC values, check cellular signal strength, press registration button 3 times on communicator
REG light stays on after registration Ensure “Send data to device” step was completed, check for cellular signal issues, verify panel programming (*29=1)
Cannot find MAC/CRC values Look on the sides and bottom of the communicator box, or check the label on the communicator itself
Service level confusion Confirm with customer which features they need before selecting service level – changing later requires contacting support
Total Connect login not working Verify email address is correct in customer profile, ensure welcome email wasn’t filtered as spam

Training Equipment Reset Guide

Back to the Top

Complete Factory Reset Procedures for Training Equipment

After completing the training exercises, use these procedures to reset all equipment for the next technician.

1. Tuxedo Touch Keypad Reset & Thermostat Transfer

1.
Exclude Z-Wave Thermostat:
From the Tuxedo Touch Home screen, tap “Devices”
Tap “Z-Wave Setup”
Select the thermostat from the device list
Tap “Remove Device”
On the thermostat, press “Menu”
Scroll to “Z-Wave” and select “Excluding” option
Wait for successful exclusion confirmation on the Tuxedo Touch
2.
Re-include Thermostat to House System:
On the house system’s Z-Wave controller, initiate the “Include Device” process
On the thermostat, press “Menu”
Scroll to “Z-Wave Setup”
Select “Set Control” to enter inclusion mode
Wait for successful inclusion confirmation
Verify the thermostat is properly functioning with the house system
Perform a Panel Sync to ensure the thermostat is fully integrated with the house system
Test temperature control functionality to confirm proper operation
3.
Factory Reset Tuxedo Touch:
From the Home screen, tap “Setup”
Enter the installer code (default 4112)
Tap “System” → “Advanced Setup”
Tap “Restore Defaults”
Tap “Reset to Factory Defaults”
Confirm by tapping “Yes”
The keypad will reboot automatically

2. Vista 20P Panel Factory Reset

1.
Enter Programming Mode:
Press [Installer Code] + 8 + 0 + 0 (default installer code is 4112)
2.
Perform Factory Reset:
Enter *97 to perform factory reset
The keypad will display “97”
Press *99 to exit programming mode
3.
Verify Reset:
Default installer code will be restored to 4112
Default master code will be restored to 1234

3. Cancel Lanvac.Mobi Account

1.
Log in to Lanvac.Mobi:
Navigate to lanvac.Mobi
Enter administrator credentials
SEE ANDI FOR DETAILS… lanvac.mobi may not be the best method to recycle accounts.
Desktop lanvac remote software is the best method to recycle accounts.
2.
Access Account:
Search for the training account number (starts with “O” O9525) You may need to toggle on “View Recycled Accounts” if you see the account after toggling the account is already recycled.
Click “View” to open the account
3.
Remove Communicator (Admin Only):
Under “Communicator Options,” locate the AlarmNet Communicator
Click the trash icon to delete the communicator
Select “Partial Cancel” or “Complete Cancel” when prompted
Note: This step handles the cellular communicator deactivation – no physical disconnection is required
4.
Deactivate Account (Admin Only):
From the account view, click “View Account”
Click “Deactivate Account”
Select reason: “Training/Test Account”
Confirm closure

4. Delete AlarmNet 360 Account

1.
Log in to AlarmNet 360:
Navigate to AlarmNet 360
Enter administrator credentials
2.
Locate Account:
Go to Menu → Programming
Search for the account using City-CS-Sub format (90-84-XXXX)
3.
Delete Account:
Click “Actions” → Select “Edit” → Click blue arrow
Select the trash icon “Delete” in the top right corner
Select: “Delete Total Connect Location”
Confirm deletion when prompted
Note: This will automatically remove all associated services including Total Connect
Select: “Delete Security Account”
Select: “Do you want to Delete this account?” Press OK to Continue.

IMPORTANT NOTE:

After completing all reset procedures, verify that all equipment has been properly reset and all accounts have been closed. This ensures the training environment is ready for the next technician and prevents any lingering test accounts from affecting production systems.

ProSeries AlarmNet 360 Account Setup – Reference Guide

Back to the Top

Password: Contact administrator
Setup Step Value/Action Explanation
Lanvac.Mobi Access Login: Contact administrator Lanvac is the central station that will monitor the alarm system. The first step is to create an account in their system before setting up the AlarmNet communicator.
Account Number Format Must start with “O”, have 4 digits, and end with a number This format is required by Lanvac’s system. The “O” prefix identifies it as a specific account type, and the 4-digit format ensures compatibility with their monitoring software.
Account Testing Put account on test for the day This prevents false alarms from being dispatched during the installation and programming process. The central station will log signals but not dispatch authorities.
ProSeries Selection Select “ProSeries” when creating account Choosing the correct panel type ensures that AlarmNet 360 configures the proper communication protocols and settings specific to the ProSeries platform.
Processing Time 1-2 minutes typical ProSeries panels typically register faster than older systems due to their integrated cellular communicator and advanced processing capabilities.
AlarmNet 360 Login Login: Contact administrator These are the company credentials for accessing the AlarmNet 360 portal. All technicians use the same login to ensure consistent account management and tracking.
Account Identification 90-84-XXXX This is the unique identifier for the alarm system in the format of “City Code, Central Station, Account Number”. This specific format ensures proper routing of alarm signals to the correct central station.
Communicator Identifiers MAC & CRC For ProSeries panels, these values are found on a label on the cellular communicator module or on the inside of the panel. They are required to register the device on the AlarmNet network.
Service Levels Security / Smart Security / Smart Home For ProSeries, Smart Home is the most common selection as it enables the integrated Z-Wave controller for smart home automation in addition to security monitoring.
Z-Wave Controller ProSeries Z-Wave Controller Unlike legacy systems that require external Z-Wave controllers, the ProSeries has an integrated Z-Wave controller built into the panel itself.
Signal Testing Via “Communications Test” menu ProSeries panels have a dedicated test menu that allows for quick communication testing without triggering actual alarms or emergency responses.

ProSeries AlarmNet 360 – Key Benefits & Features

Back to the Top

Advantages of ProSeries with AlarmNet 360

The ProSeries platform offers several significant advantages over legacy systems when used with AlarmNet 360:

Integration Benefits:

  • All-in-One Design: The ProSeries panel includes an integrated cellular communicator, Wi-Fi module, and Z-Wave controller in a single unit, eliminating the need for separate add-on modules.
  • Simplified Installation: The integrated design means fewer components to install and wire, reducing installation time and potential points of failure.
  • Dual-Path Communication: ProSeries automatically utilizes both cellular and Wi-Fi paths when available, providing redundant communication for enhanced reliability.
  • Streamlined Registration: The registration process is faster and more reliable than with legacy systems, often completing in 1-2 minutes rather than requiring multiple attempts.
  • Remote Programming: Almost all system parameters can be configured remotely through AlarmNet 360, reducing the need for service calls for minor programming changes.

End-User Experience:

  • Intuitive Interface: The ProSeries touchscreen provides a smartphone-like user experience that customers find familiar and easy to use.
  • Enhanced Total Connect Integration: ProSeries systems synchronize more seamlessly with Total Connect 2.0, including real-time status updates and comprehensive system control.
  • Advanced Automation: The integrated Z-Wave controller allows for more sophisticated automation rules, including conditional logic and multi-device scenes.
  • Smoother Updates: Firmware updates can be delivered over-the-air, ensuring the system always has the latest features and security enhancements without requiring a technician visit.
  • Voice Control: ProSeries systems support integration with popular voice assistants through Total Connect, allowing for hands-free control of security and smart home functions.

Technical Advantages:

  • Extended Wireless Range: ProSeries wireless sensors use advanced SiX technology with significantly greater range than traditional 345MHz sensors.
  • Encrypted Communications: All wireless communications between the panel and sensors are encrypted, providing a higher level of security against wireless attacks.
  • Flexible Zone Expansion: The system can support up to 128 wireless zones without requiring hardwired expanders or additional modules.
  • Battery Backup: ProSeries panels include an internal rechargeable battery backup that provides extended protection during power outages.
  • Advanced Diagnostics: The system provides comprehensive diagnostic information both on the panel and through AlarmNet 360, making troubleshooting more efficient.

Best Practices: When setting up a ProSeries system on AlarmNet 360, first ensure the panel has been powered up and initialized before beginning the AlarmNet 360 account creation process. This allows the panel to establish its cellular connection and prepare for registration. Additionally, always verify that both cellular and Wi-Fi communications are functioning properly before leaving the installation site to ensure reliable monitoring and remote access capabilities.

Common Setup Issues with ProSeries

Back to the Top
Issue Solution
Poor cellular signal strength Verify signal bars on panel (minimum 3 required). Consider repositioning the panel if signal is weak, or add an external antenna if available.
Registration failure Double-check MAC/CRC values. Ensure panel has completed startup sequence. Restart the panel and try registration again.
Wi-Fi connection issues Verify Wi-Fi credentials. Ensure router is compatible with ProSeries (2.4GHz network required). Position panel within good range of router.
Z-Wave device enrollment problems Factory reset Z-Wave device before attempting enrollment. Ensure device is within 5-10 feet of panel during initial pairing process.
Total Connect sync issues Perform system sync from Total Connect app. Verify master code in AlarmNet 360 matches panel’s master code.
Automation rules not functioning Check that all devices involved in the rule are online and responding. Verify rule conditions and actions are properly configured.
Panel software version issues Ensure panel has the latest firmware by checking System Information in Settings menu. Schedule update if needed.

Training Equipment Reset Guide

Back to the Top

Complete Factory Reset Procedures for ProSeries Training Equipment

After completing the training exercises, use these procedures to reset all equipment for the next technician.

1. ProSeries Panel Factory Reset

  1. Enter Advanced Settings:
    • From the Home screen, tap “Settings”
    • Select “Advanced Settings”
    • Enter the installer code (default: 1111 or training code: 1943)
  2. Access Reset Options:
    • Scroll down and select “Maintenance”
    • Tap “Factory Reset”
  3. Perform Reset:
    • Select “Full Reset” option (not “Preserve Programming”)
    • Enter installer code again to confirm
    • Tap “Reset” to proceed
    • Wait for panel to reboot (approximately 2-3 minutes)
  4. Verify Reset:
    • Panel will reboot and display initial setup screens
    • Default installer code will be restored to 1111
    • Default master code will be restored to 1234
    • All Z-Wave devices will be removed from the network

2. Z-Wave Device Reset

  1. Z-Wave Thermostat Reset:
    • On the thermostat, press “Menu”
    • Scroll to “Z-Wave”
    • Select “Reset” or “Factory Default”
    • Confirm reset when prompted
    • Thermostat is now ready for re-enrollment with the next system
  2. Other Z-Wave Devices:
    • Consult device manual for specific factory reset procedure
    • Most devices require pressing and holding a specific button or sequence
    • Verify reset by checking device no longer responds to panel commands

3. Cancel AlarmNet 360 & Lanvac Accounts

  1. Cancel AlarmNet 360 Account:
    • Log in to AlarmNet 360
    • Go to Menu → Programming
    • Search for the account using City-CS-Sub format (90-84-XXXX)
    • Click “Actions” → Select “Delete Account” → Click blue arrow
    • Select reason: “Training/Test Account”
    • Confirm deletion when prompted
  2. Cancel Lanvac.Mobi Account:
    • Navigate to lanvac.Mobi
    • Enter administrator credentials
    • Search for the training account number (starts with “O”)
    • Click “View” to open the account
    • Under “Communicator Options,” locate the AlarmNet Communicator
    • Click the trash icon to delete the communicator
    • From the account view, click “Close Account”
    • Select reason: “Training/Test Account”
    • Confirm closure

IMPORTANT NOTE:

After completing all reset procedures, verify that all equipment has been properly reset and all accounts have been closed. This ensures the training environment is ready for the next technician and prevents any lingering test accounts from affecting production systems.

Cross-Zoning – Practical Applications

Back to the Top

What is Cross-Zoning and Why Use It?

Cross-zoning is a powerful feature that requires two separate zones to be triggered within a specified time window before generating an alarm. This significantly reduces false alarms while maintaining security.

Real-World Applications:

  • Garage Protection: Garages are notorious for false alarms due to mice, temperature changes, or moving objects. Installing two motion detectors at different angles and cross-zoning them ensures that small animals triggering one sensor won’t cause an alarm unless the second sensor also detects movement.
  • Problematic Areas: For zones that have historically generated false alarms (near HVAC vents, areas with shifting sunlight, etc.), cross-zoning provides an excellent solution without compromising security.
  • High-Value Areas: For areas containing valuables, cross-zoning two different types of sensors (e.g., a motion detector and a glass break detector) creates a highly secure zone that’s also resistant to false alarms.
  • Large Open Spaces: In warehouses or large living areas, cross-zoning multiple motion detectors ensures that environmental factors affecting one area won’t trigger false alarms.

In This Training: We’re cross-zoning two wireless motion sensors to demonstrate how this feature works. In a real installation, these would typically be placed in the same area to monitor the same space from different angles, significantly reducing false alarms while maintaining effective security coverage.

ProSeries Advantages Over Legacy Systems

Back to the Top

Key Benefits of ProSeries Systems

The ProSeries platform offers significant advantages over legacy systems like Vista and LYNX:

  • Integrated Design: All-in-one panel with built-in touchscreen, cellular communicator, and wireless receiver reduces installation complexity and improves reliability.
  • Enhanced User Experience: Intuitive touchscreen interface with smartphone-like operation makes system management easier for customers.
  • Advanced Wireless Technology: Improved range, battery life, and signal reliability compared to older wireless systems.
  • Simplified Programming: On-screen guided programming eliminates the need for complex programming codes and keypad sequences.
  • Automatic Updates: Over-the-air firmware updates ensure the system always has the latest features and security patches.
  • Enhanced Encryption: Advanced encryption protocols provide better security against hacking attempts.
  • Flexible Communication: Dual-path communication (cellular and Wi-Fi) ensures reliable connectivity to central station.
  • Integrated Smart Home: Built-in Z-Wave Plus controller allows for seamless integration with smart home devices.

Installation Efficiency: The ProSeries system typically reduces installation time by 30-50% compared to legacy systems, allowing technicians to complete more installations per day while providing a superior customer experience.

Dahua NVR Setup & Configuration Guide

Table of Contents
  • 1. Initial Setup of Dahua NVR
  • 2. Adding IP Cameras to Dahua NVR
  • 3. Setting Date and Time Automatically
  • 4. Configuring Motion Recording
  • 5. Playback & Backup Operations
  • 6. Remote Access Setup

Initial Setup of Dahua NVR

Back to the Top

The initial setup process configures the basic parameters needed for your Dahua NVR to function properly. This includes regional settings, time configuration, and security credentials.

1

Region & Language

Select Canada as your region and English as your preferred language. Choose NTSC as the video standard for North America.

2

Time Settings

Configure time zone to (UTC-05:00) Central Time (US & Canada). Set the current date and time according to your location.

3

Daylight Savings Time

Enable Daylight Savings Time and configure it using the Week format with the specific settings for our region.

McKee Security DST Settings

Use these exact settings for Daylight Savings Time configuration:

  • DST Type: Week
  • Start Time: March, 2nd Sunday at 00:00:00
  • End Time: November, 1st Sunday at 00:00:00

Ensure these settings are applied to maintain accurate timekeeping throughout the year!

4

Software Agreement

Read through the software license agreement, check the box to indicate you agree, and click next to continue.

5

Admin Password

We always use the default username Admin with our standard password Mckeesecurity1! which meets all security requirements.

6

Network Configuration

Configure network settings by enabling DHCP for automatic IP assignment or manually setting IP address, subnet mask, and gateway.

McKee Security Standard Credentials

Always use these credentials when setting up Dahua NVR systems:

  • Username: Admin (Default – leave unchanged)
  • Password: Mckeesecurity1!

Note: This password meets all security requirements with uppercase letters, lowercase letters, numbers, and special characters.

Pro Tip: When setting up the system for clients, document the credentials in their installation paperwork. While we use our standard password for initial setup, clients may wish to change it later for their own security.

Adding IP Cameras to Dahua NVR

Back to the Top

Connecting IP cameras to your NVR allows for centralized management and recording. The Dahua NVR can automatically discover compatible cameras on the same network or you can add them manually.

  1. Log in to the NVR and navigate to Setting → Camera
  2. In the Device Search section, you’ll see all available cameras on the network
  3. If no cameras appear, click Search Device to refresh the list
  4. Check the box next to the camera you want to add
  5. Click Add to connect the camera to the NVR
  6. If the camera has the same password as the NVR, it will connect automatically
  7. If the status remains red, the camera has a different password and needs to be configured
  1. Navigate to Setting → Camera
  2. Click Manual Add at the bottom of the screen
  3. Select which channel to add the camera to
  4. Choose the manufacturer (Private for Dahua cameras, ONVIF for third-party cameras)
  5. Enter the camera’s IP address
  6. Specify the TCP port (default is usually 37777 for Dahua)
  7. Enter the username Admin and password Mckeesecurity1!
  8. Click OK to add the camera
  9. Click Refresh to verify the connection status

Important: If a camera shows a red status after adding, it typically indicates an authentication issue. Edit the camera settings and ensure you’re using the correct username and password. If all else fails, try a factory reset using the following steps: Unplug the camera, press and hold the reset button with a pin, apply power while holding the button, and wait for the IR filter to click three times. This is especially important for third-party cameras, as they will most definitely have different credentials.

Connection Method Best Used When Advantages
Automatic Discovery Cameras are on the same network as the NVR Quick setup, automatically finds all compatible cameras
Manual Addition Camera is not automatically discovered or is on a different subnet Works with any IP camera, allows for custom configuration

Setting Date and Time Automatically

Back to the Top

Accurate timestamps are crucial for surveillance footage. The NTP (Network Time Protocol) feature allows your Dahua device to synchronize with internet time servers for precise timekeeping.

1

Access Date & Time Settings

Right-click on the monitor screen, select Main Menu, enter your password, then navigate to System → General → Date and Time.

2

Set Time Zone

Select (UTC-05:00) Central Time (US & Canada) as your time zone and click Save.

3

Configure DST

Enable Daylight Savings Time and set it to start on the 2nd Sunday of March and end on the 1st Sunday of November at 00:00:00.

4

Enable NTP

Find the NTP option and enable it. Click OK when prompted for confirmation.

5

Manual Update

Click Manual Update to force an immediate time synchronization.

6

Apply Settings

Click Apply to save the configuration and implement the automatic time synchronization.

Pro Tip: For systems without internet access, you can set up a local NTP server on your network or manually adjust the time periodically to maintain accuracy. Correct time settings are especially important for Canadian installations where DST changes can affect timestamp accuracy.

Configuring Motion Recording

Back to the Top

Motion recording optimizes storage by only recording when movement is detected. This section explains how to set up motion detection and recording on your Dahua NVR.

  1. From the main menu, go to Alarm → Video Detection
  2. Select the channel you want to configure
  3. Click Setting next to Region to define the motion detection area
  4. In the live view, highlight areas where you want motion detection (red) or clear areas where you don’t want detection
  5. Adjust Sensitivity (how easily motion is detected) and Threshold (minimum amount of motion required to trigger)
  6. Click OK to save the region settings
  7. Check the box to Enable motion detection. Typically, no other settings are changed unless sensitivity issues arise.
  1. Click Setting next to Schedule to define when motion detection is active
  2. Set the schedule (default is 24/7) and click OK
  3. Leave Post Record time as default
  4. Check Recording Channel to specify which channels record when motion is detected. Typically, leave this as default.
  5. Configure additional actions if desired (snapshots, PTZ movements, audio alerts). Typically, these settings are left unchanged.
  6. Click More for additional settings like Anti-Dither, on-screen messages, email alerts, etc.
  7. Click Apply to save all settings
  1. Go to Setting → Storage → Schedule
  2. Select the channel you configured for motion detection
  3. Click Setting next to any day of the week
  4. Select All to apply settings to all days (if desired)
  5. Check both General and Motion to ensure both are recorded
  6. Set the time period (typically 00:00-24:00 for all day)
  7. Click OK to save schedule settings
  8. Yellow bars will appear across the schedule indicating motion recording times
  9. Click Apply to save all changes

Important: Both motion detection AND recording schedule must be properly configured for motion recording to work. If either is missing, the system may not record as expected.

Setting Recommended Value Effect
Sensitivity Medium-High (60-80) Higher values detect smaller movements but may increase false triggers
Threshold Medium (40-60) Lower values trigger recording with less motion but may increase false triggers
Post Record 10-30 seconds Continues recording after motion stops to capture complete events
Anti-Dither 5-10 seconds Prevents multiple triggers during continuous motion

Playback & Backup Operations

Back to the Top

Reviewing and exporting recorded footage are essential functions of any surveillance system. Dahua NVRs provide intuitive tools for playback and backup operations.

  1. From the main menu, click Search
  2. On the right side, select the cameras you want to view by checking their boxes
  3. Use the calendar to select the date (days with recordings have white dots)
  4. The timeline at the bottom shows all recordings for the selected date
  5. Filter recordings by type by checking/unchecking General, Motion, etc.
  6. Check Sync to synchronize playback across all selected channels
  7. Double-click on the timeline to start playback at that specific time
  8. Use playback controls to pause, play, stop, reverse, slow forward, or fast forward
  9. Adjust volume if the footage has audio
  1. Select Backup from the main menu
  2. Insert a USB drive into the NVR
  3. Select the USB drive from the list and note available space
  4. Format the drive if needed by clicking Format
  5. Choose the backup location by clicking Browse
  6. Select which channel(s) to back up
  7. Choose the recording type (All, General, Motion, etc.)
  8. Select stream type (Mainstream or Substream)
  9. Specify the time period using Custom for specific dates
  10. Choose file format (DAV for original format or MP4 for wider compatibility)
  11. Click Search to find matching recordings
  12. Check boxes for the footage you want to back up
  13. Click Backup to start the transfer process
  14. Wait for the backup to complete

Pro Tip: When backing up footage for clients or legal purposes, use the DAV format which automatically includes the Smart Player software on the USB drive. This allows the footage to be played on any Windows computer without installing additional software.

Backup Format Advantages Best Used For
DAV (Original) Preserves all metadata and features, includes Smart Player Evidence preservation, maintaining full quality and information
MP4 (Converted) Widely compatible with most media players and devices Sharing footage with clients or others who need easy playback

Remote Access Setup

Back to the Top

Accessing your surveillance system remotely allows you to monitor your property from anywhere. Dahua offers multiple methods for remote access, with P2P being the simplest option.

  1. During initial setup or from the main menu, navigate to the P2P settings page
  2. Ensure the P2P option is enabled
  3. Connect the NVR to a network with internet access
  4. Wait for the status to change from “Offline” to “Online”
  5. Note the QR code displayed on the screen – this will be used to add the device to the mobile app
  1. Download and install the DMSS app on your mobile device
  2. Open the app and tap the plus (+) button in the top right corner
  3. Select SN Scan to add the device via QR code
  4. Scan the QR code displayed on your NVR’s interface
  5. Choose whether to add the device to your Local app or to your DMSS Account
  6. Select NVR as the device type
  7. Enter a name for the device
  8. Input the username Admin and password Mckeesecurity1!
  9. Tap Save and then Done
  10. The app will connect to the NVR and display the live view of connected cameras
  1. Download and install Smart PSS Lite on your computer
  2. Complete the initial setup by accepting license agreements
  3. Create a password for Smart PSS (minimum 8 characters with one uppercase letter or special character)
  4. Set up security questions for password recovery
  5. Once the program initializes, click Device Manager
  6. Click Add to add a new device
  7. Enter a name for the device
  8. Change the connection type from IP domain to P2P
  9. Input your recorder’s serial number (found in the Network menu of your recorder)
  10. Enter the username Admin and password Mckeesecurity1!
  11. Click Add to complete the process
  12. Wait for the device to show as online
  13. Click Home then Live View to access your cameras

Important: Always set up devices locally to ensure they are not tied to personal accounts. This allows for easier management and access by authorized personnel.

Pro Tip: For McKee Security installations, always use our default password Mckeesecurity1! for remote access. This allows us to easily maintain and manage client systems. The system remains secure as third-party access requires both the device’s serial number and the password.

CAT5e Wiring Guide

Back to the Top

Terminating CAT5e Cable

  1. Strip approximately 1 inch of the outer jacket from the CAT5e cable using the wire stripper.
  2. Untwist and straighten the wire pairs.
  3. Arrange the wires in the following order (TIA/EIA-568A standard, from left to right):
    • White-Green
    • Green
    • White-Orange
    • Blue
    • White-Blue
    • Orange
    • White-Brown
    • Brown
  4. Trim the wires to approximately 1/2 inch length, ensuring they are even.
  5. Insert the wires into the RJ45 connector, making sure they reach the end of the connector.
  6. Using the crimping tool, crimp the connector firmly to secure the wires.
  7. Repeat the process for the other end of the cable.

Camera Installation

Back to the Top

Mounting Instructions

  1. Choose optimal camera locations that provide maximum coverage of the desired areas.
  2. Mount the camera bracket using appropriate screws for the mounting surface.
  3. Route the CAT5e cable to the camera location, ensuring it’s protected from weather and damage.
  4. Connect the CAT5e cable to the camera’s network port.
  5. If not using PoE, connect the power supply to the camera.
  6. Adjust the camera angle for optimal viewing coverage.
  7. Secure all connections and ensure the camera is properly weather-sealed.

NVR Installation

Back to the Top

Setup Instructions

  1. Choose a secure location for the NVR, preferably in a climate-controlled area.
  2. Mount the NVR using appropriate mounting hardware.
  3. Connect the NVR power supply to a reliable power source.
  4. Connect the camera CAT5e cables to the NVR’s PoE ports (if using PoE) or network ports.
  5. Ensure all connections are secure and properly organized.
  6. Power on the NVR and verify all connections are working properly.

Starlink Installation & Configuration Guide

Table of Contents
  • 1. Initial Setup & Site Assessment
  • 2. Hardware Installation
  • 3. Dish Positioning & Mounting
  • 4. Router Setup & Configuration
  • 5. Network Testing & Optimization
  • 6. Troubleshooting Common Issues

Initial Setup & Site Assessment

Back to the Top

Before installing Starlink hardware, a proper site assessment is crucial to ensure optimal performance. This includes checking for obstructions and planning the installation location.

1

Download the Starlink App

Install the Starlink App on your mobile device from the App Store or Google Play Store. This app is essential for setup, obstruction checking, and system management.

2

Check for Obstructions

Use the app’s “Check for Obstructions” feature to scan the sky and identify potential obstacles like trees, buildings, or power lines that might block the satellite signal.

3

Select Installation Location

Choose a location with a clear view of the northern sky (in the northern hemisphere). The dish needs approximately 100° field of view with no obstructions.

Optimal Installation Requirements

For best performance, ensure your installation site meets these criteria:

  • Clear View: Unobstructed view of the sky in the direction Starlink needs to point
  • Proximity to Power: Within 100 feet of a power outlet
  • Stable Mounting Surface: Secure location that won’t move or vibrate

Remember: Even minimal obstructions can significantly impact service quality!

Pro Tip: The Starlink dish requires a clear view of the sky to communicate with satellites. Even small obstructions like tree branches can cause interruptions. When in doubt, choose a higher mounting position or consider removing obstacles.

Hardware Installation

Back to the Top

Proper unpacking and assembly of Starlink components is essential for a successful installation. Follow these steps to ensure all hardware is correctly set up.

  1. Carefully unbox all Starlink components
  2. Verify that you have all necessary parts:
    • Starlink Dish (Dishy)
    • Base/mounting hardware
    • Router
    • Power supply
    • Cables
  3. Check for any visible damage to components
  4. Keep all packaging materials until installation is complete
  1. Plan the cable route from dish to router location
  2. Measure the distance to ensure cable length is sufficient
  3. Consider using conduit for outdoor cable runs to protect from weather
  4. For roof installations, identify entry point for cable
  5. Ensure cable is not pinched, kinked, or excessively bent
  6. Leave some slack in the cable to account for dish movement
  7. Use appropriate cable clips or ties to secure cable along its path

Important: The Starlink cable is specifically designed for this system and cannot be cut, spliced, or replaced with standard Ethernet cable. Handle with care and plan your installation to accommodate the fixed cable length. If you need a longer cable, you must purchase the official Starlink cable extension.

Component Installation Considerations Best Practices
Starlink Dish Requires clear view of sky, power connection Mount securely, allow for automatic positioning
Router Indoor placement, central to coverage area Keep away from other electronics, ensure good ventilation
Cables Weather exposure, routing challenges Use conduit for protection, avoid sharp bends

Dish Positioning & Mounting

Back to the Top

Proper mounting of the Starlink dish is critical for reliable service. The dish must be securely installed with a clear view of the sky to maintain connection with satellites.

1

Site Assessment

Use the Starlink app’s obstruction scanner tool to check for potential obstructions. Rotate your phone’s camera to scan the sky and find a location with clear views, typically the peak of your roof is best.

2

Prepare for Installation

Gather required tools: impact drill with appropriate hex nut runner (14mm or 9/16″), and the included mounting hardware from your Starlink kit.

3

Mount Installation

Install the wall mount securely to your chosen location, ensuring proper alignment and weatherproofing of all penetrations.

  1. Position the mount at your chosen location on the fascia board
  2. Apply silicone sealant around the mounting area to prevent water intrusion
  3. Place the lag bolt in the hex nut runner attached to your impact drill
  4. Drive the first lag bolt directly into the fascia board, screwing it in almost all the way
  5. Slide the mount onto this bolt and align with the top hole
  6. Insert and tighten the second lag bolt through the mount using the impact drill
  7. Fully tighten both lag bolts to secure the mount
  8. Insert the mast into the mount base until it snaps into place
  9. Route the cable through the mast and mount as follows:
    • Feed cable through the notch at the bottom of the mast
    • Push cable up through the mast
    • Feed cable down through the hole at the top of the wall mount
  1. Remove the kickstand from the Gen 3 dish by folding it down, lifting the tab, and pulling straight out
  2. Connect the cable to the dish, ensuring the moisture seal is flush
  3. Slide the mounting bracket into place until it snaps securely
  4. Place the dish onto the installed mast, ensuring it seats properly
  5. Pull any excess cable through the mount to eliminate slack
  6. Install the cover to complete the mount installation
  1. Power on the Starlink system and allow 10-15 minutes for boot-up
  2. Open the Starlink app and connect to the dish’s WiFi
  3. If you see a “Starlink misaligned” message, tap it to access the alignment tool
  4. Loosen the adjustment knob on the mount
  5. Rotate the dish while watching the real-time alignment target on the app
  6. Once properly aligned (typically facing north/northwest), tighten the adjustment knob
  7. Verify alignment is successful in the app

Important: The Gen 3 Starlink dish requires manual alignment as it doesn’t have motors to automatically position itself. Once aligned, the adjustment knob should be tightened to prevent movement. Note that the mount only accommodates a 4-inch roof overhang, so for typical homes, mounting directly to the fascia board is recommended.

Pro Tip: After installation, use the included cable clips to secure the cable along its path down the wall. Create a drip loop before the cable enters your home to prevent water from following the cable inside. For additional protection, consider using the Starlink cable routing kit for a cleaner, more weather-resistant installation.

Router Setup & Configuration

Back to the Top

Once the dish is installed, setting up the Starlink router properly ensures optimal network performance and connectivity for all devices.

1

Connect Power

Connect the power supply to the Starlink router and plug it into a power outlet. Ensure the dish cable is securely connected to the power brick.

2

Wait for Initialization

Allow 10-15 minutes for the system to boot up, position the dish, and establish connection with satellites.

3

Connect to WiFi

Find the default network name and password on the bottom of the router. Connect your device to this network.

  1. Open the Starlink app on your mobile device
  2. Follow the on-screen instructions to set up your network
  3. Create a custom WiFi network name (SSID)
  4. Set a strong password for your network
  5. Configure advanced settings if needed:
    • Split 2.4GHz and 5GHz networks if desired
    • Enable/disable guest network
    • Configure port forwarding if needed
  6. Save your settings and reconnect to the new network
  1. If using your own router, put Starlink router in bypass mode:
    • Open Starlink app
    • Go to Settings > Network
    • Enable Bypass Mode
  2. Connect your third-party router to the Auxiliary port on the Starlink router
  3. Configure your router according to manufacturer instructions
  4. Set up WiFi networks and security on your router
  5. Test connectivity through your router

Network Security Best Practices

Always follow these guidelines when setting up your Starlink network:

  • WiFi Password: Use a strong, unique password with at least 12 characters
  • Network Name: Avoid using identifying information in your SSID
  • Admin Access: Change the default admin password for router management

Note: Document all credentials in a secure location for future reference.

Network Testing & Optimization

Back to the Top

After installation, testing your Starlink connection helps verify performance and identify any issues that need addressing.

  1. Use the built-in speed test in the Starlink app to check connection performance
  2. Verify download speeds (typically 50-150+ Mbps)
  3. Check upload speeds (typically 10-20+ Mbps)
  4. Note latency values (typically 20-40ms)
  5. Run tests at different times of day to understand performance variations
  6. Compare results with Starlink’s expected performance for your area
  1. Check WiFi signal strength throughout the property
  2. Identify dead zones or weak signal areas
  3. Reposition router if needed for better coverage
  4. Consider adding mesh nodes for larger properties
  5. Test connectivity with various devices (phones, laptops, smart TVs)
  6. Optimize router placement:
    • Central location
    • Elevated position
    • Away from interference sources

Important: Starlink performance can vary based on satellite coverage, weather conditions, and network congestion. Set appropriate expectations with clients regarding typical performance variations. Brief outages during satellite handoffs are normal and typically last only seconds.

Performance Metric Typical Range Factors Affecting Performance
Download Speed 50-150+ Mbps Satellite coverage, obstructions, network congestion
Upload Speed 10-20+ Mbps Satellite coverage, obstructions, network congestion
Latency 20-40ms Satellite position, atmospheric conditions
Packet Loss 0-1% Obstructions, weather, satellite handoffs

Troubleshooting Common Issues

Back to the Top

Even with proper installation, Starlink systems may encounter issues. This section covers common problems and their solutions.

  1. Check the Starlink app for outage notifications
  2. Review obstruction data in the app to identify potential blockages
  3. Verify all cable connections are secure
  4. Inspect the dish for physical damage or debris
  5. Check for new obstructions that may have developed (tree growth, new structures)
  6. Perform a system reboot:
    • Unplug power for 20 seconds
    • Reconnect power
    • Allow 10-15 minutes for full restart
  1. Check for interference from other electronic devices
  2. Verify router placement is optimal
  3. Separate high-bandwidth devices to 5GHz network
  4. Update router firmware if available
  5. Test with wired connection to isolate WiFi issues from satellite issues
  6. Consider using WiFi analyzer apps to identify channel congestion
  7. Reset router to factory settings if persistent issues occur
  1. Check for error messages or LED indicators on the router
  2. Inspect all components for physical damage
  3. Verify proper power supply to all components
  4. Check for overheating issues (ensure proper ventilation)
  5. Test with alternative power outlets
  6. Contact Starlink support for hardware replacement if needed

Pro Tip: The Starlink app provides detailed statistics about your connection, including outages, obstructions, and signal quality. Use the “Statistics” page to diagnose issues and understand system performance. The “Obstruction Map” feature can help identify exactly which parts of the sky are being blocked.

Important: Do not attempt to disassemble any Starlink components. The system contains no user-serviceable parts, and opening components will void the warranty. For serious hardware issues, contact Starlink support for assistance or replacement.

Proper Dish Positioning

  1. Take the dish outside to a location with:
    • Clear, unobstructed view of the sky (no trees, buildings, etc.)
    • Within cable reach of where the router will be placed
  2. Deploy the kickstand on the back of the dish
  3. Position the dish so it’s level and stable
  4. Ensure the dish is not placed:
    • Under trees or overhangs
    • Near metal objects that could cause interference
    • Where it could be disturbed or damaged
  5. Run the cable back to where the router will be located

App Configuration

Starlink App Setup

  1. Download the Starlink app on your smartphone (iOS or Android)
  2. Plug in the power to the router (with dish already connected and positioned)
  3. Wait for the router to power on (approximately 1-2 minutes)
  4. Open the Starlink app and follow the setup wizard:
    • Select “Set up Starlink”
    • Choose “Gen 3 Dish” when prompted
    • Follow the app instructions to verify dish placement
    • Connect to the temporary Starlink WiFi network
  5. Configure WiFi settings:
    • Set SSID name to: Training Starlink
    • Set password to: Mckeesecurity1!
    • You can choose to split 2.4GHz and 5GHz networks or keep them combined
  6. Wait for the dish to:
    • Boot up completely
    • Search for satellites
    • Download any firmware updates
    • Align itself for optimal reception
  7. This process may take 20-30 minutes to complete

Testing Connection

Back to the Top

Verifying Connectivity

  1. Once the Starlink shows “Online” status in the app:
    • Connect a device to the “Training Starlink” WiFi network
    • Verify you can browse the internet
  2. Run a speed test:
    • Use the built-in speed test in the Starlink app
    • Alternatively, use speedtest.net or fast.com
    • Typical speeds should be between 100-300 Mbps download
  3. Explore the Starlink app features:
    • Statistics tab shows connection details
    • Check for any obstructions reported
    • Review network settings
  4. Test the Ethernet ports on the router (if needed):
    • Connect a device via Ethernet cable
    • Verify wired connection works properly

Factory Reset

Back to the Top

Resetting After Training

  1. After completing your training, reset the Starlink router:
    • Locate the small reset button on the bottom of the router
    • Use a paperclip or similar tool to press and hold the reset button
    • Hold for 15 seconds until the router light begins flashing
    • Release the button and wait for the router to reset (5-10 minutes)
  2. Alternatively, factory reset through the app:
    • Open the Starlink app
    • Go to Settings → Advanced → Factory Reset
    • Confirm the reset when prompted
  3. After reset, the Starlink will:
    • Return to default settings
    • Clear all custom WiFi settings
    • Be ready for the next training session
  4. Properly pack all components back in the box:
    • Carefully fold the dish cable
    • Collapse the kickstand on the dish
    • Return all components to their original packaging
