Understanding UniFi Basics

Back to the Top

UniFi is Ubiquiti’s enterprise network management ecosystem that allows you to build scalable networks with centralized control. Before diving into specific equipment, it’s essential to understand the four core components that make up any UniFi network.

1

Gateway

The gateway (router/firewall) connects your local network to the internet. It handles routing between your Wide Area Network (WAN) and Local Area Network (LAN), providing security and network management.

2

Network Controller

The UniFi Network Controller is the management software that configures and monitors your entire network. It can be hosted on a gateway device, cloud service, or installed locally on a computer.

3

Switches

Network switches provide wired connectivity for your devices. UniFi offers various switch models with different port counts and Power over Ethernet (PoE) capabilities to power devices like access points.

4

Access Points

Wireless access points (APs) provide WiFi connectivity. UniFi offers various models with different coverage capabilities, speeds, and features to suit different environments.

The UniFi Advantage

What makes UniFi special is its unified management system through the UniFi Network Controller. Key benefits include:

  • Single Dashboard: Manage all network devices from one interface
  • Scalability: Start small and add devices as needed
  • Remote Management: Configure your network from anywhere via unifi.ui.com
  • No Licensing Fees: Pay only for hardware, with free software updates

UniFi allows you to build enterprise-grade networks with home-user simplicity!

Pro Tip: Many UniFi devices combine multiple components in a single unit. For example, the Dream Machine Pro includes a gateway, network controller, and switch, while the UniFi Express combines a gateway, access point, and controller. Understanding which components are included in each device will help you plan your network more effectively.

Gateway Selection & Controller Options

Back to the Top

The gateway and controller are the central components of any UniFi network. We offer various deployment options based on the specific needs of each installation.

For our professional installations, we typically recommend these gateway options:

  • Dream Machine Pro (UDM Pro):
    • Rack-mountable enterprise gateway
    • Integrated UniFi OS with controller
    • Support for multiple UniFi applications (Network, Protect, Access, Talk)
    • 10Gbps SFP+ WAN and LAN ports
    • Ideal for larger homes and business installations
    • Enhanced security features with IDS/IPS
  • UniFi Dream Machine (UDM):
    • All-in-one solution with built-in access point
    • Compact design for residential installations
    • Integrated controller, gateway, and 4-port switch
    • Perfect for smaller deployments where space is limited
  • Cloud Gateway Ultra:
    • Compact gateway with built-in controller
    • Good option for installations where rack space is unavailable
    • 4-port switch for basic wired connectivity

We offer multiple controller deployment options to suit different client needs:

1. Locally Hosted Controller

For many residential installations, we deploy a locally hosted controller on the client’s computer:

  • Advantages:
    • No additional hardware costs
    • Can be installed on Windows, macOS, or Linux
    • Good option when client has a computer that is regularly powered on
    • Full control over your own data
  • Considerations:
    • Computer must be running for configuration changes
    • Statistics only collect when controller is running
    • Local network access only (unless manually configured for remote access)
  • Setup Process:
    • Download and install UniFi Network Application on client computer
    • Run initial setup wizard to configure the network
    • Adopt devices through the controller interface

2. Cloud Gateway with Integrated Controller

For installations where 24/7 access and remote management are important:

  • Advantages:
    • Always-on controller that’s accessible remotely
    • Continuous statistics collection
    • Automatic notifications for network events
    • Easy access through unifi.ui.com
  • Considerations:
    • Additional hardware cost
    • Requires dedicated power and space

3. Cloud Hosted Controller

For clients who need cloud management without additional hardware:

  • Advantages:
    • No need for on-premises controller hardware
    • Accessible from anywhere
    • Managed by Ubiquiti or third-party hosting services
  • Considerations:
    • Monthly hosting fees may apply (with third-party services)
    • Requires stable internet connection for management

Our Approach: For most residential installations, we recommend the locally hosted controller for its simplicity and cost-effectiveness. For more complex setups or clients who need constant remote access, we suggest a Dream Machine Pro or UDM with integrated controller.

Important: When using a locally hosted controller, we ensure clients understand that the computer needs to be running when network changes are needed. We also set up automatic backups of the controller configuration to prevent data loss.

Pro Tip: For clients with locally hosted controllers who still want remote access, we can configure port forwarding and Dynamic DNS to enable secure remote management of their network.

Network Switch Options

Back to the Top

Network switches provide wired connectivity for devices. Our company offers flexible approaches to switch selection based on client needs and budget considerations.

We deploy both UniFi and standard (non-Ubiquiti) switches depending on the installation requirements:

UniFi Switches

  • Advantages:
    • Full integration with the UniFi controller
    • Centralized management and monitoring
    • Detailed port statistics and traffic analysis
    • VLAN configuration through the controller interface
    • Remote power cycling of individual ports
  • Best For:
    • Installations requiring advanced network management
    • Clients who need detailed network analytics
    • Deployments with complex VLAN configurations
    • Environments where remote management is crucial

Standard Network Switches

  • Advantages:
    • Lower cost in many cases
    • No adoption process required
    • Simple plug-and-play operation
    • Works well with UniFi access points using PoE injectors
    • Wide availability of different brands and models
  • Best For:
    • Budget-conscious installations
    • Simple networks without complex management needs
    • Residential deployments with basic requirements
    • Situations where existing non-UniFi switches are already installed

Our Approach: For many residential installations, we often utilize standard network switches paired with PoE injectors for access points. This approach is cost-effective while still providing excellent performance. For more complex installations or clients who need advanced management features, we recommend UniFi switches for their integration benefits.

For powering UniFi access points and other PoE devices, we often utilize PoE injectors rather than PoE switches:

PoE Injectors

  • Advantages:
    • Cost-effective solution when powering a limited number of devices
    • Can be used with any standard switch
    • Allows for deploying high-power devices without upgrading the entire switch
    • Easy to replace if a single injector fails
  • Installation Approach:
    • We typically place injectors near the network switch or equipment rack
    • Each device receives its own dedicated injector
    • We ensure proper labeling for easy identification
    • We use proper power management to keep installations tidy

PoE Switches

  • When We Recommend Them:
    • Installations with numerous PoE devices (typically 4+ devices)
    • Environments where cable management and aesthetics are critical
    • Deployments requiring remote power management of devices
    • Enterprise installations with rack-mounted equipment

Our Standard Practice: For most residential installations, we use quality standard switches coupled with appropriate PoE injectors. This provides the best balance of performance and cost-effectiveness while maintaining flexibility for future upgrades.

Pro Tip: When using PoE injectors, we always match the injector’s power output to the device requirements. The U7 Pro access point, for example, requires a PoE+ (802.3at) compatible injector to function properly. Using an underpowered injector can lead to device instability or failure.

Wireless Access Points

Back to the Top

Wireless access points (APs) provide WiFi connectivity for your network. UniFi offers various models to suit different coverage needs, environments, and budgets.

For professional installations, we recommend high-performance access points that provide excellent coverage and support for numerous client devices:

  • U7 Pro (Our Primary Recommendation):
    • Latest WiFi 7 technology for future-proof installations
    • Superior coverage and client capacity
    • Ideal for both residential and business environments
    • Best performance for bandwidth-intensive applications
    • Support for more simultaneous clients
  • U6 Pro:
    • Excellent WiFi 6 performance for mid-range installations
    • Good option when cost is a consideration but performance is still important
    • Appropriate for medium-sized homes or offices
  • U6 Mesh:
    • Weatherproof design for outdoor installations
    • Extends network coverage to outdoor areas
    • Ideal for patios, pools, and outdoor living spaces

When selecting access points for a project, we consider the size of the space, the number of clients, types of usage (streaming, gaming, video conferencing), and building construction materials.

Proper placement of access points is crucial for optimal WiFi coverage:

  • Start with Less: Begin with one or two strategically placed access points and add more only if needed
  • Central Placement: Position APs centrally in the areas needing coverage
  • Ceiling Mounting: When possible, mount APs on ceilings for best coverage
  • Avoid Obstructions: Minimize walls, especially brick or concrete, between APs and client devices
  • AP Spacing: In larger homes, place APs to provide overlapping coverage without excessive interference
  • Test and Adjust: After installation, test coverage throughout your space and adjust placement if needed

For most standard homes (2000-3000 sq ft), one or two well-placed access points should provide excellent coverage. Brick or concrete construction might require additional APs.

Important: When it comes to access points, more is not always better. Too many access points in close proximity can cause interference and actually reduce network performance. Start with fewer access points and add more only if you identify specific areas with poor coverage after testing.

Pro Tip: If you need outdoor WiFi coverage, the U6 Mesh is specifically designed for this purpose with weatherproofing and flexible mounting options. Place it under an eave or on an exterior wall facing your outdoor space for the best results.

Access Point Model Best Use Case Approximate Coverage
U7 Pro High-density environments, future-proof installations 3000-3500 sq ft
U6 Pro Larger homes or device-dense environments 2500-3000 sq ft
U6 Plus Mid-sized residential installations 2000-2500 sq ft
U6 Lite Smaller spaces with fewer devices 1500-2000 sq ft
U6 Mesh Outdoor coverage 2000-2500 sq ft (outdoor)

Building Your Network

Back to the Top

With an understanding of the core components, you can now build a UniFi network that meets your specific needs. Here are recommended configurations for different scenarios.

For a standard professional residential installation (2000-3000 sq ft), we typically recommend:

  1. Gateway: Dream Machine Pro or UniFi Dream Machine (UDM)
    • Provides robust routing, firewall, and integrated network controller
    • Built-in switch ports for directly connected devices
    • Support for advanced security features
  2. Controller: Locally installed on client’s computer (or integrated in gateway)
    • Cost-effective management solution
    • Full control over network configuration
  3. Switch: Standard non-UniFi switch
    • Provides wired connectivity for various devices
    • Compatible with PoE injectors for access points
  4. Access Points: 1-2 U7 Pro APs
    • Provides future-proof WiFi 7 coverage throughout the home
    • Strategically placed for optimal coverage
    • Powered via PoE injectors connected to the switch

This setup provides excellent performance with our recommended professional-grade components while maintaining flexibility for future expansion.

For a small apartment or condo (under 1500 sq ft), a simpler setup may be appropriate:

  1. All-in-One Option: UniFi Dream Machine (UDM)
    • Combines gateway, controller, switch, and access point
    • Multiple LAN ports for connecting wired devices
    • Good WiFi coverage for smaller spaces
  2. Alternative: Cloud Gateway Ultra with a U7 Pro AP
    • More customizable solution with better wireless performance
    • Good option when superior WiFi performance is needed

These compact solutions provide excellent performance in smaller spaces while maintaining professional quality.

For larger homes (3000+ sq ft) or homes with difficult construction (brick, concrete, multiple levels):

  1. Gateway: Dream Machine Pro
    • More powerful processing for larger networks
    • Rack-mountable for professional installation
    • Enhanced security features and expansion options
  2. Switch: Combination of standard switches and UniFi switches where appropriate
    • More ports for additional wired devices
    • Strategic use of PoE switches where multiple PoE devices are clustered
    • Standard switches with injectors in other locations
  3. Access Points: 3-4 U7 Pro APs
    • Strategically placed throughout the home
    • Higher capacity for more connected devices
    • Better handling of bandwidth-intensive applications
  4. Outdoor: U6 Mesh for yard/patio coverage
    • Weather-resistant for outdoor placement
    • Extends coverage to outdoor living spaces

This advanced setup provides comprehensive coverage for larger spaces with many connected devices while maintaining our professional installation standards.

Physical Network Layout

A typical UniFi network will have this physical layout:

  1. Internet modem connects to the WAN port on your UniFi gateway
  2. Gateway connects to your primary switch (or built-in switch ports are used for limited connections)
  3. Switch connects to:
    • Access points (via PoE if available)
    • Wired client devices (computers, printers, etc.)
    • Additional switches in other locations if needed
  4. Access points provide wireless connectivity for mobile devices

Always plan cable runs carefully, using proper Ethernet cabling (Cat6 recommended) and considering future expansion needs.

Network Configuration

Back to the Top

Our professional installation process includes proper configuration of all UniFi network components to ensure optimal performance, security, and reliability for our clients.

Our standard configuration process for client installations includes:

  1. Initial Assessment:
    • Evaluate client requirements and network usage patterns
    • Perform wireless environment scan to identify potential interference
    • Determine optimal access point placements
  2. Controller Setup:
    • Install UniFi Network Application on client’s computer (for locally hosted controllers)
    • Create secure admin credentials and enable backups
    • Document all settings for future reference
  3. Device Adoption:
    • Connect and adopt all UniFi devices to controller
    • Update firmware to latest stable versions
    • Assign logical device names for easy identification
  4. Network Optimization:
    • Configure appropriate subnets based on network size
    • Implement VLANs when needed for network segmentation
    • Optimize radio settings for access points (channels, power, band steering)
  5. Post-Installation Testing:
    • Verify coverage throughout the property with heat mapping
    • Test throughput at various locations
    • Validate proper functioning of all network services

Security is a priority in all our installations. Our standard security configurations include:

  1. Firewall Setup:
    • Configure appropriate inbound and outbound rules
    • Block common attack vectors
    • Implement geo-blocking as needed to prevent foreign access attempts
  2. Guest Network:
    • Create isolated guest networks with appropriate bandwidth limitations
    • Implement guest portal with custom branding when requested
    • Configure automatic guest user expiration
  3. IoT Segmentation:
    • Create separate networks for IoT devices when appropriate
    • Restrict IoT devices from accessing other network segments
    • Maintain internet access for cloud-connected devices
  4. Intrusion Prevention:
    • Enable and configure Intrusion Detection System (IDS) and Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)
    • Balance security with performance based on client needs
    • Set up alerts for suspicious activities

For clients with specific requirements, we implement advanced networking features:

  1. Remote Access VPN:
    • Configure L2TP, OpenVPN, or WireGuard VPN for secure remote access
    • Create client-specific profiles with appropriate permissions
    • Document connection procedures for client reference
  2. Quality of Service (QoS):
    • Implement traffic shaping rules for critical applications
    • Prioritize video conferencing and VoIP traffic
    • Limit bandwidth for non-essential services during peak usage
  3. Multi-WAN Configuration:
    • Set up failover or load balancing for installations with multiple internet connections
    • Configure appropriate failover detection and alerting
    • Optimize traffic routing based on connection quality
  4. Site-to-Site VPN:
    • Connect multiple locations securely for clients with multiple properties
    • Implement appropriate encryption and authentication
    • Configure routing between sites for seamless resource access

Client Documentation and Training

Every installation includes comprehensive documentation and client training:

  • Network Documentation: Detailed records of all network settings, credentials, and configurations
  • Network Diagram: Visual representation of the network topology for future reference
  • Training Session: Personalized guidance on basic network management tasks
  • Support Information: Clear instructions on how to reach our support team when needed

This documentation ensures that clients understand their network and can perform basic management tasks while having a clear path for professional support when needed.

Pro Tip: For clients with locally hosted controllers, we recommend creating scheduled backups of the controller configuration to an external drive or cloud storage service. This prevents configuration loss in case of computer issues and allows for quick recovery if needed.

Factory Reset (Initial)

Back to the Top

Resetting Cloud Gateway Before Training

Since the cloud gateway may have been previously configured, we need to reset it to factory defaults before beginning training.

  1. Reset Cloud Gateway:
    • Locate the reset button (usually a small pinhole on the back or bottom)
    • Using a paperclip or similar tool, press and hold the reset button for 10 seconds
    • Release when the LED indicators start flashing
    • Wait approximately 5 minutes for the device to fully reset and reboot
  2. Verification:
    • After resetting, the gateway should show solid white or blue LED indicators
    • The cloud gateway will broadcast its default SSID (typically “UniFi_” followed by letters/numbers)

Note: The access points used for training are typically new and don’t require factory reset at the beginning of training.

Important: The factory reset process will erase ALL configuration settings and return the gateway to its original state. This is necessary for training purposes to ensure a clean starting point.

Cloud Gateway Setup

Back to the Top

Primary Method: Cloud Gateway Configuration

  1. Physical Setup:
    • Connect the cloud gateway to your internet modem/router via the WAN port
    • Connect power to the cloud gateway
    • Wait for the device to boot completely (approximately 2-3 minutes)
  2. Initial Configuration:
    • Connect to the default WiFi network broadcast by the gateway (name shown on device)
    • Open a web browser and navigate to http://setup.ui.com or http://unifi.ui.com
    • Alternatively, use the UniFi Network mobile app to set up the device
    • Follow the setup wizard to create admin credentials
  3. Basic Network Configuration:
    • Name your device and set up the location
    • Configure internet connection settings (usually automatic/DHCP)
    • Create your main WiFi network with appropriate security settings
    • Wait for initial configuration to complete
  4. Dashboard Access:
    • After initial setup, you’ll be directed to the UniFi OS dashboard
    • Access the UniFi Network application from the dashboard
    • Make note of the admin credentials for future configuration steps

Pro Tip: During the setup process, you’ll be prompted to create or sign in with a Ubiquiti account. For training purposes, you can skip this step by selecting the option to set up without a Ubiquiti account.

Cloud Gateway vs. Laptop Controller Comparison

Understanding the differences between the two controller options:

Feature Cloud Gateway (Recommended) Laptop Controller (Alternative)
Hardware Dedicated UniFi hardware device remains onsite Technician’s laptop used temporarily for setup only
Controller Presence Always present on customer’s network Only present during initial setup, then removed
Network Integration Functions as router/gateway + controller APs operate in “self-hosted” mode after setup
Post-Setup Management Continuous management through gateway interface Basic changes require technician to return with controller laptop
Data Collection Continuous data collection and statistics No ongoing statistics collection after setup
Updates Automatic firmware updates for all devices Manual updates require technician visit with controller laptop
Best For Networks needing ongoing management and monitoring Simple networks with basic needs where cost is a primary concern

How the Laptop Controller Method Works:

  1. Technician brings company laptop with UniFi Network Application installed
  2. Laptop connects to the customer’s network during setup
  3. APs are configured and adopted through the laptop controller
  4. Once configuration is complete, the laptop controller is disconnected and removed
  5. APs continue to function with the settings they were given – in “standalone” mode
  6. For future changes, the technician must return with the controller laptop

Recommendation: The cloud gateway approach provides continuous management, monitoring, and the ability for customers to make changes themselves. The laptop controller approach is more cost-effective for simple installations where ongoing changes are unlikely and advanced features aren’t needed.

Network Hardware Setup

Back to the Top

Setting Up Your Network Hardware

  1. Internet Connection Setup:
    • Connect an ethernet cable from your internet modem/router to the WAN port on the cloud gateway
    • Verify internet connectivity LED indicators on the gateway
  2. 8-Port Non-PoE Switch Setup:
    • Connect power to the switch
    • Connect an ethernet cable from one of the LAN ports on the cloud gateway to any port on the switch
    • Verify link lights are active on the connected ports
  3. Prepare PoE Injectors:
    • Identify the “IN” (data) and “OUT” (data + power) ports on each 48V Ubiquiti injector
    • Connect ethernet cables from the switch to the “IN” port of each injector
    • Leave the “OUT” ports disconnected until the access points are in position
    • Do not connect power to the injectors yet
  4. Cable Management:
    • Label cables appropriately for easy identification
    • Organize cables neatly to avoid tangling
    • Keep power and data cables separated when possible

Important: The Ubiquiti U7 Pro access points require 802.3at PoE+ power (48V). Ensure your PoE injectors are properly rated to provide sufficient power. Insufficient power can cause device instability or prevent proper operation.

UniFi vs. Non-UniFi Switches

It’s important to understand the differences between Ubiquiti UniFi switches and standard non-UniFi switches like the one we’re using:

Feature Non-UniFi Switch (Current Setup) UniFi Switch
Controller Integration Not visible in the UniFi controller Fully integrated with detailed statistics and controls
Adoption Process Not required – simple plug and play Must be formally adopted in the UniFi controller
Configuration Standalone or via manufacturer’s interface Configured through the UniFi controller
PoE Management Basic or no power monitoring Detailed per-port power monitoring and control
VLAN Support Depends on make/model (manual configuration) Easy VLAN configuration through controller
Firmware Updates Manual through manufacturer’s process Centralized updates through controller

Note: Using standard non-UniFi switches with PoE injectors is a valid and cost-effective approach for many installations. UniFi access points work well with any standard switch, though you lose the integrated management capabilities.

Access Point Installation

Back to the Top

Installing U7 Pro Access Points

  1. Access Point Placement:
    • For training purposes, place the two U7 Pro access points in the designated training area
    • In real installations, you would mount APs on ceilings or high walls for optimal coverage
    • Maintain adequate distance between APs to prevent overlap and interference
    • Ensure ethernet cables can reach from PoE injectors to each AP
  2. Physical Connection:
    • Connect an ethernet cable from each PoE injector’s “OUT” port to each access point
    • Ensure the cables are securely connected
    • Connect power to the PoE injectors
    • Verify the power indicator LEDs on the access points illuminate
  3. Initial Power-Up:
    • Wait for the access points to completely boot (approximately 1-2 minutes)
    • When ready for adoption, the LEDs will show a specific pattern (usually solid white or blue)
    • Note the MAC addresses from the AP labels for easier identification during adoption

Pro Tip: For training purposes, we’re setting up both APs in the same room, but in a real installation, you would distribute them throughout the property for optimal coverage. A single U7 Pro can effectively cover 3000-3500 square feet in ideal conditions.

U7 Pro Specifications

  • WiFi Standard: WiFi 7 (802.11be)
  • Frequency Bands: Tri-band (2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, 6 GHz)
  • Maximum Throughput: Up to 10.8 Gbps total
  • MIMO: 4×4 MU-MIMO
  • Power Requirements: 802.3at PoE+ (48V)
  • Power Consumption: Up to 25W
  • Ethernet Port: 1x 2.5GbE
  • Coverage Area: Approximately 3000-3500 sq ft per AP (optimal conditions)
  • Concurrent Clients: 700+

Network Configuration

Back to the Top

Adopting Access Points

  1. Access the UniFi Controller:
    • On a connected device, open a web browser and navigate to https://unifi.ui.com
    • Log in with the admin credentials created during cloud gateway setup
    • Select your gateway from the site list
  2. Adoption Process:
    • Go to the “Devices” section in the controller
    • Look for the U7 Pro access points in the “Ready to Adopt” section
    • Click “Adopt” for each access point
    • Wait for the adoption process to complete – this can take a few minutes
    • The devices will update firmware if necessary (which adds additional time)
  3. Device Naming:
    • Once adopted, click on each access point in the Devices list
    • In the right sidebar that appears, click on “Config”
    • Change the device name to something descriptive (e.g., “AP1-Training”, “AP2-Training”)
    • Click “Apply Changes” to save the new names

Creating Wireless Networks

  1. Create Main Network:
    • In the UniFi controller, navigate to “Settings” → “WiFi”
    • Click “Add New” or “Create New WiFi Network”
    • Enter the Name/SSID: Training Wi-Fi
    • Enter the Password: McKeesecurity1!
    • Security Protocol: WPA2/WPA3
    • Network: Default
    • Leave other settings at their defaults
    • Click “Add” or “Save” to create the network
  2. Create Guest Network:
    • Click “Add New” or “Create New WiFi Network” again
    • Enter the Name/SSID: Training Guest
    • Enter the Password: McKeesecurity1!
    • Security Protocol: WPA2/WPA3
    • Click “Advanced” to show more options
    • Check the box for “Guest Network”
    • Network: Select the guest network created earlier (if available)
    • Enable “Client Device Isolation” to prevent guest devices from seeing each other
    • Click “Add” or “Save” to create the guest network

Advanced Access Point Configuration

  1. Access Point Radio Settings:
    • In the Devices section, click on an access point
    • In the right sidebar, click on “Config” then “Radios”
    • For 2.4 GHz radio:
      • Set TX Power to Medium
      • Set Channel Width to HT20
      • Set Channel to Auto (or a specific channel if directed)
    • For 5 GHz radio:
      • Set TX Power to Medium
      • Set Channel Width to VHT80
      • Set Channel to Auto (or a specific channel if directed)
    • For 6 GHz radio (U7 Pro only):
      • Set TX Power to Medium
      • Set Channel Width to HE160
      • Set Channel to Auto
    • Click “Apply Changes” to save the settings
  2. Band Steering (Optional):
    • Go to “Settings” → “WiFi” → click on your main network
    • Enable “Band Steering” to encourage clients to use 5 GHz when available
    • Click “Apply Changes” to save
  3. Mesh Configuration (If Using Multiple APs):
    • Mesh networking is automatically enabled between UniFi access points
    • No additional configuration is necessary for basic mesh functionality
    • Wired backhaul is always preferred when ethernet connections are available

Pro Tip: For training purposes, we’re using simple network names and passwords. In real-world installations, you would use client-specific network names and strong, unique passwords.

Testing & Verification

Back to the Top

Verifying Network Performance

  1. Basic Connectivity Test:
    • Connect a smartphone or laptop to the “Training Wi-Fi” network
    • Verify you can browse the internet
    • Confirm the device shows up in the “Clients” section of the controller
  2. Guest Network Test:
    • Connect a different device to the “Training Guest” network
    • Verify internet access is available
    • Try to access a device on the main network – this should fail due to isolation
  3. Speed Test:
    • Use the built-in speed test in the UniFi controller
    • Go to the main Dashboard and click “Run Speed Test”
    • Record the download and upload speeds
  4. AP Performance Check:
    • In the Devices section, click on each access point
    • Review the client connections, channel utilization, and interference
    • Look for any warnings or alerts that might indicate problems

Network Monitoring Tools

  1. Dashboard Overview:
    • The main Dashboard provides a quick overview of network health
    • Check the client count, data usage, and any alerts
    • Look at the Devices panel to ensure all hardware is online and healthy
  2. Network Topology:
    • Go to the “Topology” view to see how devices are connected
    • Verify that access points are connected to the correct network
    • Check for any unexpected connections or configurations
  3. Client Devices:
    • View the “Clients” section to see connected devices
    • Check which access point each client is connected to
    • Review signal strength and connection quality
  4. Insights and Statistics:
    • Explore the “Insights” section for more detailed network statistics
    • Review traffic patterns and bandwidth usage
    • Identify any potential issues or bottlenecks

Important: For training purposes, we’re testing in a controlled environment. In real-world installations, you should perform additional tests including signal coverage throughout the entire property, high-bandwidth application testing, and multi-client stress testing.

Factory Reset (Final)

Back to the Top

Resetting Devices After Training

Once training is complete, all devices should be reset to factory defaults in the correct order to prepare them for future training sessions.

  1. Reset Access Points First:
    • Log into the controller (cloud gateway)
    • Go to “Devices” and select each access point
    • Click “Settings” then “Manage Device” and select “Factory Default”
    • Confirm the reset for each device
    • Wait for each access point to complete its reset (2-3 minutes)
  2. Manual AP Reset (if controller access is unavailable):
    • With the AP powered on, locate the reset button (usually on the back or underside)
    • Press and hold the reset button for 10 seconds
    • Release when the LED begins flashing
    • The AP will reset and reboot (2-3 minutes)
  3. Reset Cloud Gateway Last:
    • Only after all access points have been reset
    • From the UniFi OS dashboard, go to “Settings” then “System”
    • Select “Factory Default” at the bottom of the page
    • Confirm the reset and wait for the device to reboot
    • Alternatively, use the hardware reset button as described in initial reset
  4. Equipment Storage:
    • Disconnect all cables and power supplies
    • Properly organize and store all components
    • Return equipment to designated storage location

Important: Always reset access points FIRST, and cloud gateway LAST. This ensures proper cleanup of the training environment. Ensure all devices are properly reset before concluding the training session.
